segments.text: Course tutor: Oh, Karen.
Course tutor: Hi, come in, take a seat.
Course tutor: I wanted to talk to you about this assignment you've handed in.
Course tutor: I'll give it back to you with detailed feedback, but first there are a few things I'd like to run over.
Course tutor: You know pointers for next time.
Course tutor: When you hand in your next assignment, can you ensure that you've gone through these aspects?
Karen: Hum, sure, will do.
Course tutor: Now, the first thing is your literature review.
Course tutor: It was not sufficient enough so I would like to see your book reports to get your complete view.
Course tutor: So can you submit those with the next assignment?
Course tutor: Then I can find out which parts you've chosen to leave out.
Karen: Oh, yes.
Karen: Sorry, I didn't realise you needed them.
Karen: It did cross my mind that what I've handed in is a bit thin, but then I started to work on another project and forgot to revise it.
Course tutor: That's alright.
Course tutor: And I've found some errors, just small ones where you had quoted people but not recorded the information properly at the end.
Course tutor: Don't forget to go through and make sure that your references are accurate.
Course tutor: They were very relevant, though.
Course tutor: Just check and revise them using the format sample that I've emailed you beforehand, OK?
Karen: Yes, I'll remember to check them.
Course tutor: Now, you made some good points but it might be helpful if you could include a few other examples, just to drive your point home.
Course tutor: Don't start writing more paragraphs; just slot them in at the end of what you've already written.
Course tutor: Now the thing you have to do, if you really want to get a better mark, is expand the ideas you're presenting.
Course tutor: Then your argument becomes more valid.
Karen: OK.
Karen: Hum, can I talk to you about this presentation I've got to do?
Course tutor: Sure, of course.
Karen: When shall I stage what we've studied?
Karen: Am I doing it next term?
Karen: I can't remember what we said.
Course tutor: Well, the thing is, Marco couldn't do it, so you agreed to do it at the next seminar.
Course tutor: So how can I help?
Karen: Um … actually, I'm wondering what you suggest to be the key part of the presentation.
Karen: I mean, which part should I spend most of my time on?
Course tutor: That's a question to the point.
Course tutor: Well, you have very little time really, so it's absolutely essential for you to explain the experiment.
Course tutor: Of course, you'll have a summary in the handouts you give out but you can still start the presentation with that.
Course tutor: It's optional, though.
Karen: And do I have to give you the abstract first, or shall I just email it to all the students?
Course tutor: Uh, no.
Course tutor: I do need to see it first and see if it needs to be polished up.
Course tutor: And then we'll get some printouts done.
Course tutor: Now, they'll need to be done by 3rd December, so I'll need to see it by 26th November if that's OK?
Karen: Yes, fine.
Course tutor: Oh, and I need to talk to you about where the presentation is going to be made.
Course tutor: Given the actual number of participants and audience, we've had problems with the rooms because we'll need something bigger than usual.

Course tutor: In our faculty, the only room available is the computer room, which is far from suitable considering the layout of the room, so we'll have to go across the road and do it in the chemistry lab.
Course tutor: It's big enough to accommodate everyone and is equipped with the proper overhead projector in there as well.
Karen: OK, right.
Karen: And I get a grade for this, don't I?
Course tutor: Yes, your first one was graded by your tutor, but this one will be scored by the professor.
Course tutor: But relax, the criteria will be consistent, and you'll get an unbiased result.
Karen: Oh, and I've sorted out my modules for next year.
Karen: You asked me to tell you about my choices.
Course tutor: Yes, what've you decided?
Karen: Oh, it was really hard to determine.
Karen: Hum, I've already done the Data Collection once, so that wasn't really a choice.
Karen: I couldn't make up my mind between Language and Society and Communication Skills.
Karen: I read the syllabuses but they sound more or less the same to me.
Karen: Anyway, I went for Communication Skills in the end because I know the lecturer.
Karen: Actually, Social Interaction seems to cover much the same ground, so I didn't bother with that either.
Karen: Hum, I thought Discourse Analysis looked really interesting.
Karen: It studies and analyses the texts beyond the literal meaning of the sentences.
Karen: It looks into the connotation beyond the sentences.
Karen: And in fact, it also covers a little bit of Research Methodology in it, so I thought I'd do that, rather than the full methodology course.
Karen: Kill two birds with one stone as it were.
Karen: And then I fancied something drastically different, so I thought Psycholinguistics would be interesting, unless you think it'll be more worthwhile for me to do the Phonology course?
Course tutor: No, I think you've made sensible choices.
Course tutor: I'm glad you're organised.
Course tutor: OK, let's meet again in a couple of weeks to see how you're getting on.
Karen: OK, thank you.
Karen: See you then.
